title: Sample Efficient Deep Reinforcement Learning via Uncertainty Estimation

paragraphs.text:
Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

## - SAMPLE EFFICIENT DEEP REINFORCEMENT LEARN ING VIA UNCERTAINTY ESTIMATION

**Vincent Mai, Kaustubh Mani and Liam Paull** _[∗]_ Robotics and Embodied AI Lab Mila - Quebec Institute of Artificial Intelligence Universit´e de Montr´eal, Quebec, Canada _{_ vincent.mai,kaustubh.mani,liam.paull _}_ @umontreal.ca

## ABSTRACT

In model-free deep reinforcement learning (RL) algorithms, using noisy value estimates to supervise policy evaluation and optimization is detrimental to the sample efficiency. As this noise is heteroscedastic, its effects can be mitigated using uncertainty-based weights in the optimization process. Previous methods rely on sampled ensembles, which do not capture all aspects of uncertainty. We provide a systematic analysis of the sources of uncertainty in the noisy supervision that occurs in RL, and introduce inverse-variance RL, a Bayesian framework which combines probabilistic ensembles and Batch Inverse Variance weighting. We propose a method whereby two complementary uncertainty estimation methods account for both the Q-value and the environment stochasticity to better mitigate the negative impacts of noisy supervision. Our results show significant improvement in terms of sample efficiency on discrete and continuous control tasks.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Deep reinforcement learning (DRL) methods have proven to be powerful at solving sequential decision-making tasks across domains (Silver et al., 2016; OpenAI et al., 2019). Combining the flexibility of the reinforcement learning framework with the representational power of deep neural networks enables policy optimization in complex and high-dimensional environments with unknown dynamics models to maximize the expected cumulative reward (Sutton & Barto, 2018).

An important limitation of DRL methods is their sample inefficiency: an enormous amount of data is necessary and makes training expensive. This makes applying DRL in the real world challenging, for example in robotics (S¨underhauf et al., 2018; Dulac-Arnold et al., 2019). In tasks like manipulation, sample collection is a slow and costly process (Liu et al., 2021). It is even more expensive in riskaverse applications like autonomous driving (Kothari et al., 2021).

Among the current state-of-the-art approaches to improve learning efficiency, a promising direction is to exploit the prevalence of uncertainty in the underlying DRL algorithm. By adopting a Bayesian framework, we can consider the sampled quantities in DRL as random variables and leverage information about their distributions to improve the learning process (Osband et al., 2018). In this paper, we consider the particular problem of unreliable supervision in the temporal difference update and the policy optimization process. In DRL, value predictions are used to supervise the training: in temporal difference-based algorithms, they are included in bootstrapped target values which are used as labels; in actor-critic frameworks, the policy is trained to optimize them. That these value predictions are noisy slows the learning and brings instability (Kumar et al., 2019; 2020). The amount of noise in the supervision depends on the uncertainty of the value prediction, which evolves during the training process and depends on the state (and action) evaluated. It is therefore _heteroscedastic_ .

While there is an extensive body of literature focused on using the uncertainty of the value prediction to guide the exploration/exploitation trade-off (Dearden et al., 1998; Strens, 2001; Osband et al., 2016; Pathak et al., 2017; Chen et al., 2017; Osband et al., 2018; Fortunato et al., 2019; Osband et al., 2019; Flennerhag et al., 2020; Clements et al., 2020; Jain et al., 2021; Aravindan & Lee, 2021), there are very few works focused on leveraging it to mitigate the impact of unreliable supervision.

Distributional RL (Bellemare et al., 2017) considers the value function as a distribution to be learned as such. It is orthogonal to our proposition: we consider the uncertainty of the labels used to learn a scalar value function. In the offline RL setting, where the dataset is limited, uncertainty-weighted actor-critic (UWAC) (Wu et al., 2021) uses inverse-variance weighting to discard out-of-distribution state-action pairs using Monte Carlo dropout (Gal & Ghahramani, 2016) for uncertainty estimation.

Closer to our work, Lee et al. (2021) propose SUNRISE, in which each sample of the Bellman backup in the TD update step is weighted to lower the importance of the targets which have a high standard deviation. The weights _w_ ( _s[′] , a[′]_ ) are computed based on a sigmo¨ıd of the negative standard deviation _Q_[ˆ] std( _s[′] , a[′]_ ) scaled by a temperature hyperparameter _T_ , and then offset such that they are between 0.5 and 1: _w_ ( _s, a_ ) = _σ_ ( _−Q_[ˆ] std( _s[′] , a[′]_ ) _∗ T_ ) + 0 _._ 5. The uncertainty of the target is estimated by sampled ensembles. While SUNRISE proposes other contributions such as an exploration bonus, the heuristic weighting scheme and the limitations of sampled ensembles in capturing the predictive uncertainty leave space for improvement in the mitigation of the effects of unreliable supervision.

We propose inverse-variance reinforcement learning (IV-RL). IV-RL also uses weights to reduce the importance of uncertain targets in training. It does so by addressing the problem from two viewpoints. First, we use variance networks (Kendall & Gal, 2017), whose loss function for regression is the negative log-likelihood instead of the L2 distance. For a given state-action pair ( _s, a_ ), the network learns the target’s noise, due for example to the stochasticity of the environment or the update of the policy. It then naturally down-weights the highly noisy samples in the training process. Second, we use variance ensembles (Lakshminarayanan et al., 2017) to estimate the uncertainty of the target due to the prediction of _Q_ ( _s[′] , a[′]_ ) during the temporal-difference update. We merge the predicted variances of several variance networks through a mixture of Gaussians, which has been shown to be a reliable method to capture predictive uncertainty (Ovadia et al., 2019). We then use Batch Inverse-Variance (BIV) (Mai et al., 2021), which has been shown to significantly improve the performance of supervised learning with neural networks in the case of heteroscedastic regression. BIV is normalized, which makes it ideal to cope with different and time-varying scales of variance. We show analytically that these two different variance predictions for the target are complementary and their combination leads to consistent and significant improvements in the sample efficiency and overall performance of the learning process.

In summary, our contribution is threefold:

1. We present a systematic analysis of the sources of uncertainty in the supervision of modelfree DRL algorithms. We show that the variance of the supervision noise can be estimated with two complementary methods: negative log-likelihood and variance ensembles.

2. We introduce IV-RL, a framework that accounts for the uncertainty of the supervisory signal by weighting the samples in a mini-batch during the agent’s training. IV-RL uses BIV, a weighting scheme that is robust to poorly calibrated variance estimation.[1]

3. Our experiments show that IV-RL can lead to significant improvements in sample efficiency when applied to Deep Q-Networks (DQN) (Mnih et al., 2013) and Soft-Actor Critic (SAC) (Haarnoja et al., 2018).

In section 2, we introduce BIV as a weighting scheme for heteroscedastic regression, and variance ensembles as an uncertainty estimation method. We analyse the sources of uncertainty in the target in section 3, where we also introduce our IV-RL framework. We finally present our experimental results in section 4.

Batch Inverse-Variance (BIV) weighting (Mai et al., 2021) leverages the additional information _σk_[2][,] which is assumed to be provided, to learn faster and obtain better performance in the case of heteroscedastic noise on the labels. Applied to L2 loss, it optimizes the neural network parameters _θ_ using the following loss function for a mini-batch _D_ of size _K_[2] :

This is a normalized weighted sum with weights _wk_ = 1 _/_ ( _σk_[2][+] _[ ξ]_[)][.][Normalizing in the mini-batch] enables control of the effective learning rate, especially in cases where the training data changes over time, such as in DRL. By focusing on the relative scale of the variances instead of their absolute value, it also provides robustness to poor scale-calibration of the variance estimates.

As explained in Mai et al. (2021), _ξ_ is a hyperparameter that is important for the stability of the optimization process. A higher _ξ_ limits the highest weights, thus preventing very small variance samples from dominating the loss function for a mini-batch. However, by controlling the discrimination between the samples, _ξ_ is also key when the variance estimation is not completely trusted. It provides control of the effective mini-batch size _EBS_ , according to:

For example, imagine a mini-batch where most samples have very high variances, and only one has a very low variance. If _ξ_ = 0, this one low-variance sample is effectively the only one to count in the mini-batch, and _EBS_ tends towards 1. Increasing _ξ_ would give more relative importance to the other samples, thus increasing _EBS_ . With a very high _ξ_ compared to the variances, all weights are equal, and _EBS_ tends towards _K_ ; in this case, the BIV loss tends towards _L_ 2.

**Tuning the** _ξ_ **parameter** The simplest way to set _ξ_ is to choose a constant value as an additional hyperparameter. However, the best value is difficult to evaluate a priori and can change when the profile of variances changes during a task, as is the case in DRL.

It is instead possible to numerically compute the value of _ξ_ which ensures a minimal _EBS_ for each mini-batch. This method allows _ξ_ to automatically adapt to the different scales of variance while ensuring a minimal amount of information from the dataset is accounted for by the algorithm. The minimal _EBS_ is also a hyper-parameter, but it is easier to set and to transfer among environments, as it is simply a fraction of the original batch size. As such, it can be set as a batch size ratio.

## 2.2 ESTIMATING THE UNCERTAINTY OF A NEURAL NETWORK PREDICTION

The predictive uncertainty of a neural network can be considered as the combination of aleatoric and epistemic uncertainties (Kendall & Gal, 2017). Aleatoric uncertainty is irreducible and characterizes the non-deterministic relationship between the input and the desired output. Epistemic uncertainty is instead related to the trained model: it depends on the information available in the training data, the model’s capacity to retain it, and the learning algorithm (H¨ullermeier & Waegeman, 2021). There is currently no principled way to quantify the amount of task-related information present in the input, the training data, or the model. The state of the art for predictive uncertainty estimation instead relies on different sorts of proxies. These sometimes capture other elements, such as the noise of the labels, which we can use to our advantage. We focus here on the relevant methods to our work.

## 2.2.1 SAMPLED ENSEMBLES

Several networks independently train an ensemble of size _N_ that can be interpreted as a distribution over predictions. The expected behaviour is that different networks will only make similar predictions if they were sufficiently trained for a given input. The sampled variance of the networks’ outputs is thus interpreted as epistemic uncertainty. It is possible to include a random Bernoulli mask

of probability _p_ to each training sample, to ensure that each network undergoes different training. This method, used by Clements et al. (2020) and Lee et al. (2021), has the same principle as single network Monte-Carlo dropout (Gal & Ghahramani, 2016). As the variance is sampled, the standard deviation is usually on the same scale as the prediction.

When used at the very beginning of the training process, sampled ensembles present one particular challenge: as the networks are initialized, they all predict small values. The initial variance, instead of capturing the lack of knowledge, is then underestimated. To address this problem, Randomized Prior Functions (RPFs) enforce a prior in the variance by pairing each network with a fixed, untrained network which adds its predictions to the output (Osband et al., 2019). RPFs ensure a high variance at regions of the input space which are not well explored, and a lower variance when the trained networks have learned to compensate for their respective prior and converge to the same output. The scale of the prior is a hyper-parameter.[3]

## 2.2.2 VARIANCE NETWORKS

With variance networks, the uncertainty is predicted using loss attenuation (Nix & Weigend, 1994; Kendall & Gal, 2017). A network outputs two values in its final layer given an input _x_ : the predicted mean _µ_ ( _x_ ) and variance _σ_[2] ( _x_ ). Over a minibatch _D_ of size _K_ , the network parameters _θ_ are optimized by minimizing the negative log-likelihood of a heteroscedastic Gaussian distribution:

Variance networks naturally down-weight the labels with high variance in the optimization process. The variance prediction is trained from the error between _µθ_ ( _x_ ) and _y_ ( _x_ ). Hence, noise on the labels or changes in the regression task over time will also be captured by loss attenuation. As the variance is predicted by a neural network, it may not be well-calibrated and may be overestimated (Kuleshov et al., 2018; Levi et al., 2020; Bhatt et al., 2021). This can (1) give wrong variance estimates but also (2) affect the learning process by ignoring a sample if the variance estimate is too high.

## 2.2.3 VARIANCE ENSEMBLES

Variance ensembles, or deep ensembles (Lakshminarayanan et al., 2017), are ensembles composed of variance networks. The predictive variance is given by a Gaussian mixture over the variance predictions of each network in the ensemble. This method, when trained, is able to capture uncertainty more reliably than others (Ovadia et al., 2019), with _N_ = 5 networks in the ensemble being sufficient. Similarly to sampled ensembles, variance ensembles suffer from underestimated early epistemic variance estimation. However, compared to variance networks, they seem empirically less prone to calibration issues (1) in the final variance estimation because the mixture of Gaussians dampens single very high variances, and (2) in the learning process because even if one network does not learn correctly, the others will.
For better readability, we will use the terms var-network and var-ensemble in the rest of the paper.

## 2.3 UNCERTAINTY AND EXPLORATION IN DRL

While our work focuses on using uncertainty estimates to mitigate the impact of unreliable supervision, we can take advantage of the structure in place to better drive the exploration/exploitation trade-off. In particular, we used BootstrapDQN (Osband et al., 2016) for exploration. In this method, a single network is sampled from an ensemble at the beginning of each episode to select the action. This method is improved with the previously described RPFs (Osband et al., 2018). In continuous settings, we instead followed Lee et al. (2021) and added an Upper Confidence Bound (UCB) exploration bonus based on uncertainty prediction. As the variance in UCB is added to _Q_ -values, it must be calibrated: we evaluate it with sampled ensembles. Possible interactions between exploration strategies and IV-RL are discussed in Appendix H.

3Using RPFs in the context of DRL can destabilize the exploration due to a significant initial bias added to the value of each state-action. It must be compensated for with uncertainty-aware exploration, such as Bootstrap (Osband et al., 2016), where it leads to a significant improvement in performance for exploration-based tasks.

Assuming a zero mean and normally distributed noise, this underlying stochasticity of the generating process is well-captured by a var-network with a loss attenuation using the negative log-likelihood formulation described in equation (3).

## 3.3 ALGORITHMS

We have adapted IV-RL to DQN and SAC to produce IV-DQN and IV-SAC. Comprehensive pseudocode, as well as implementation details for each algorithm, can be found in appendix A.

## 4 RESULTS

We have tested IV-RL across a variety of different environments, using IV-DQN for discrete tasks and IV-SAC for continuous tasks. To determine the effects of IV-RL, we compare its performance to the original DQN and SAC, but also the ensemble-based baselines upon which IV-DQN and IV-SAC were built: BootstrapDQN and EnsembleSAC. We also include SUNRISE (Lee et al., 2021).

All ensemble-based methods use an ensemble size of _N_ = 5. Unless specified otherwise, each result is the average of runs over 25 seeds: 5 for the environment _×_ 5 for network initialization. The hyperparameters are the result of a thorough fine-tuning process explained in appendix B. As it uses ensembles, IV-RL is computationally expensive: computation time is discussed in Appendix D.

## 4.1 IV-DQN

We tested IV-DQN on discrete control environments selected to present different characteristics. From OpenAI Gym (Brockman et al., 2016), LunarLander is a sparse reward control environment and MountainCar is a sparse reward exploration environment. From BSuite[6] (Osband et al., 2020), Cartpole-Noise is a dense reward control environment. The BootstrapDQN baseline follows Osband et al. (2018) as explained in section 2.3. The reported SUNRISE results are obtained by applying the SUNRISE weights to BootstrapDQN. The learning curves are shown in figure 2, where the results are averaged on a 100 episode window.

IV-DQN significantly outperforms BootstrapDQN and SunriseDQN on control-based environments. However, in the exploration-based Mountain-Car where the challenge is to find the state that produces some reward, there is very little additional information in every other state for IV-DQN to improve over BootstrapDQN. All weighting schemes thus perform comparably.

In Figure 3 we perform an ablation study for LunarLander and Cartpole-Noise. In L2 + VarEnsembleDQN, the loss function _L_ BIV in equation 10 is replaced by the _L_ 2 loss. In BIV + BootstrapDQN, sampled ensembles are used instead of var-ensembles, and trained only with _L_ BIV. More details about these ablations are found in appendix A. In both cases, the use of BIV or var-ensembles improves the performance, and their combination leads to the best sample efficiency. In CartpoleNoise, var-ensembles carry most of the improvement, maybe because the low amount of states leads to low epistemic uncertainty. We also show that using one single var-network as opposed to var-ensembles is sub-optimal. This is likely due to the unstable nature of the single uncertainty estimate, which affects the learning process. More details are shown in appendix E and G. The use of var-ensembles stabilizes the variance estimation, as explained in section 2.2.3.

## 4.2 IV-SAC

IV-SAC was applied to different continuous control environments from OpenAI Gym (Brockman et al., 2016) as implemented by MBBL (Wang et al., 2019). EnsembleSAC is a baseline using ensembles with an UCB exploration bonus (Lee et al., 2021). Table 2 shows the average return after 100k and 200k steps on 25 seeds. We note that the addition of an ensemble instead of a single network, along with the uncertainty-based exploration bonus, already allows the performance to increase compared to SAC. Except in Ant, the SUNRISE weights do not seem to lead to consistently better results. In comparison, IV-SAC leads to significant improvements in performance.

Similarly to IV-DQN, we can separate the contribution from BIV and loss attenuation, as shown in the two first plots of figure 5. While both BIV and var-ensembles alone have a significant impact in Walker, it’s only their combination that brings the most improvement in HalfCheetah. Similarly to the discrete control case, a simple var-network leads to a small improvement over SAC, which is discussed in appendix G. We also show in figure 5 that the BIV weights provided with var-ensemble variance estimations than the SUNRISE weights, or even the inverse variance weights of UWAC (Wu et al., 2021). The normalization of BIV allows it to better cope with changes in the variance predictions, as seem in appendices E and F.
Figure 5: **Ablation Study:** (first two figures) Impact of using different uncertainty estimation methods (last two figures) Comparing different weighting schemes with var-ensembles.

## 5 CONCLUSION

We present Inverse Variance Reinforcement Learning (IV-RL), a framework for model-free deep reinforcement learning which leverages uncertainty estimation to enhance sample efficiency and performance. A thorough analysis of the sources of noise that contribute to errors in the target motivates the use of a combination of Batch Inverse Variance (BIV) weighting and variance ensembles to estimate the variance of the target and down weight the uncertain samples in two complementary ways. Our results show that these two components are beneficial and that their combination significantly improves the state of the art in terms of learning efficiency, in particular for control tasks. We applied IV-RL to DQN and SAC, but it can be adapted to any model-free algorithm. Modelbased, active or continuous learning could also benefit from the underlying ideas presented in this paper. We thus believe that IV-RL is a significant step to enable the application of DRL to real-world problems such as robotics, where sample efficiency is the main challenge.

## ETHICS STATEMENT

In this work, we present a method to enhance the sample efficiency of deep reinforcement learning algorithms. As such, it is agnostic to the applications, and per se does not raise any particular ethical issue. We however strongly encourage the user of our algorithm to ensure they have carefully thought about the ethical issues related to the particular field of application, such as medicine, robotics, communication, finance, etc.
As environmental sustainability can also be considered an ethical issue (Universit´e de Montr´eal, 2018), we publish the carbon footprint of our work.

Experiments were conducted using a private infrastructure, which has a carbon efficiency of 0.028 kgCO2eq/kWh. A cumulative of 12367 days, or 296808 hours, of computation was mainly performed on the hardware of type RTX 8000 (TDP of 260W). We assume full power usage of the GPUs, although this was not always the case.
Total emissions are estimated to be 2160.76 kgCO2eq of which 0 percents were directly offset. This is equivalent to 8730 km driven by an average car, or 1.08 metric tons of burned coal.
Estimations were conducted using the MachineLearning Impact calculator presented in Lacoste et al. (2019).

coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.label: Coarse (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.overall_feedback:
This paper introduces Inverse-Variance Reinforcement Learning (IV-RL), a method for improving sample efficiency in model-free DRL by weighting Bellman updates using uncertainty estimates derived from both variance networks and ensembles. By combining Batch Inverse-Variance (BIV) weighting with loss attenuation, the authors attempt to mitigate the impact of noisy supervisory signals in both discrete and continuous control tasks.

The paper addresses a significant challenge in DRL sample efficiency with a sound, albeit computationally heavy, methodology. The empirical results demonstrate clear improvements over baselines in several benchmarks, though the reliance on complex uncertainty estimation methods raises questions about scalability and the validity of the variance decomposition.

Overview issues:

- Unclear Justification for Variance Decomposition: Equation (9) decomposes the target variance into two distinct terms: one attributed to epistemic uncertainty (via ensembles) and one to aleatoric/policy-induced noise (via loss attenuation). However, the paper does not rigorously justify why these two sources of error should be treated as additive in the BIV loss. In practice, the 'predictive variance' captured by ensembles and the 'noise' captured by loss attenuation are highly correlated, as both are influenced by the agent's progress in learning the value function. This overlap risks double-counting uncertainty, potentially leading to over-conservative weighting. The authors should provide a more formal justification for this additive split or perform an ablation that demonstrates the sensitivity of the final performance to the ratio of these two terms.

- Computational Overhead and Scalability: The methodology relies on maintaining an ensemble of five variance networks for every agent. Given that the paper highlights potential real-world applications like robotics, the computational cost of training and inferring from five networks simultaneously is a significant barrier. The paper lacks a detailed analysis of the training time per environment step compared to standard SAC or Rainbow. Without a clear discussion on the trade-off between sample efficiency and wall-clock time, the claim that this method is a step toward real-world robotics is premature. The authors should include a comparison of wall-clock training time across all baselines to provide a realistic assessment of the method's efficiency.

- Sensitivity to Hyperparameter $\xi$ and $\lambda$: The BIV loss introduces a stability hyperparameter $\xi$, and the combined loss introduces $\lambda$. While the paper mentions that $\lambda$ is typically optimal around 5 or 10, it does not provide a systematic sensitivity analysis for these parameters across different environments. In RL, where reward scales vary significantly, a fixed $\lambda$ might not be sufficient to balance the two loss components effectively. If the performance is highly sensitive to these values, the method's generalizability is limited. A more robust evaluation would involve showing how performance degrades when these hyperparameters are swept across a range of values.

- Calibration of Uncertainty Estimates: The authors acknowledge that variance networks can be poorly calibrated but rely on the assumption that BIV's normalization mitigates this. However, if the variance estimates are systematically biased (e.g., consistently underestimating uncertainty early in training), the weighting might actively hinder learning by assigning high weight to incorrect targets. The paper lacks a diagnostic analysis of the variance estimates themselves—for example, plotting the predicted variance against the actual squared TD error over the course of training. Demonstrating that the variance estimates are actually tracking the error magnitude would significantly strengthen the paper's claims.

- Need for a controlled synthetic experiment to validate additive variance decomposition: The core assumption that target variance can be decomposed into additive epistemic and aleatoric components (Equation 9) is central to the IV-RL loss. However, these components are likely correlated in practice, and the paper lacks a synthetic experiment—such as a simple 1D regression task with known heteroscedastic noise and controlled epistemic uncertainty—to demonstrate that this additive split correctly identifies and weights the noise sources. Without such a controlled environment, it is difficult to determine if the performance gains are due to the proposed decomposition or simply the regularization effect of the weighting scheme. I suggest implementing a synthetic regression task where the ground-truth noise variance and the epistemic uncertainty (via model capacity/data density) are known, and verifying that the IV-RL weights converge to the optimal inverse-variance weights.

- Lack of empirical evidence for the BIV normalization's robustness to miscalibration: The authors claim that BIV is robust to poorly calibrated variance estimates, yet they provide no evidence that the BIV normalization actually corrects for the systemic bias known to exist in variance networks. If the variance estimates are consistently biased, the normalization might simply shift the scale of the weights without correcting the relative importance of samples. To substantiate this claim, the authors should plot the correlation between the learned variance $\sigma^2(s,a)$ and the actual squared TD error $|T - \hat{Q}|^2$ over the course of training for both a standard variance network and the IV-RL framework. Showing that the IV-RL weighting leads to a higher rank correlation between predicted uncertainty and actual error would demonstrate the claimed robustness.

- Missing analysis of the impact of the target-network update lag on uncertainty: In DRL, the target $T(s,a)$ relies on a slowly moving target network $\bar{Q}$. The paper assumes that the variance of the target is well-captured by the ensemble at the current training step, but it ignores the fact that the target network itself is a lagging, stale estimate of the value function. This lag introduces a non-stationary bias that is not captured by standard aleatoric or epistemic uncertainty definitions. The paper needs to discuss how this target-network lag affects the variance estimates, perhaps by comparing the performance of IV-RL with and without target-network updates, or by analyzing the variance of the target network's predictions over the course of a single update cycle.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Provide a rigorous justification or empirical analysis for the additive assumption in Equation (9) to address potential double-counting of uncertainty.

- Include a wall-clock time comparison between IV-RL and baseline methods to quantify the computational cost of the ensemble approach.

- Add a diagnostic analysis showing the correlation between predicted variance and actual TD error to verify the calibration of the uncertainty estimates.

- Include a sensitivity analysis for the hyperparameters $\lambda$ and $\xi$ to demonstrate the robustness of the proposed loss function across different reward scales.

comments.title: Inaccurate description of Gaussian mixture variance
comments.quote: The predictive variance is given by a Gaussian mixture over the variance predictions of each network in the ensemble.
comments.explanation: The text incorrectly equates the predictive variance of a deep ensemble to a simple mixture of variance predictions. For an ensemble of N networks, the predictive distribution is a mixture of Gaussians, and by the law of total variance, the total variance is the mean of the individual variances plus the variance of the individual means. The current text omits the epistemic component (the variance of the means), which is the primary feature of ensemble-based uncertainty. Please revise this to clarify that the predictive variance includes both the mean of the individual variances and the variance of the individual means.

comments.title: Inaccurate description of RPF mechanism
comments.quote: RPFs ensure a high variance at regions of the input space which are not well explored, and a lower variance when the trained networks have learned to compensate for their respective prior and converge to the same output.
comments.explanation: The description of the RPF mechanism is imprecise. In the RPF framework, the ensemble variance decreases because the learned components f_i converge to values that cancel out their respective fixed random priors f_bar_i, not because the total predictions converge to the same value. The current text suggests the variance is a measure of convergence of the total output, which obscures the role of the fixed prior in maintaining diversity. Please rewrite this to explain that the variance decreases when the learned components have successfully compensated for their respective fixed priors.

comments.title: Inconsistency in MountainCar performance reporting
comments.quote: All weighting schemes thus perform comparably.
comments.explanation: The text claims that IV-DQN and BootstrapDQN perform comparably on MountainCar, but Table 1 shows a performance gap. The 50th percentile for BootstrapDQN is 149 episodes, while for IV-DQN it is 163. The 75th percentile for BootstrapDQN is 206, while for IV-DQN it is 200. These values indicate that IV-DQN is slower to solve the environment than the baseline. Please revise the text to accurately reflect the results in Table 1 rather than claiming parity.

comments.title: Inconsistent claim regarding performance in Ant environment
comments.quote: Except in Ant, the SUNRISE weights do not seem to lead to consistently better results.
comments.explanation: The text claims SUNRISE does not lead to consistently better results except in Ant, yet Table 2 shows that at 200k steps, SUNRISE (1462 ± 154) outperforms IV-SAC (1281 ± 101) in the Ant environment. The claim that IV-SAC leads to significant improvements is contradicted by the data in the table for this specific environment. Please revise the text to accurately reflect the performance comparison shown in Table 2.

comments.title: Ambiguous definition of complementary uncertainty methods
comments.quote: We propose a method whereby two complementary uncertainty estimation methods account for both the Q-value and the environment stochasticity
comments.explanation: The abstract claims the methods account for 'Q-value' and 'environment stochasticity' separately. However, in standard RL, the Q-value is a function of the environment stochasticity. If the methods are meant to capture epistemic and aleatoric uncertainty, the current phrasing is misleading because the Q-value is the target being estimated, not a source of uncertainty. Please clarify the distinction between these two methods in the methodology section to ensure the reader understands what each component specifically estimates.

comments.title: Missing constraint on variance estimates
comments.quote: es. When these variances can be estimated, each sample is a triplet $\left(x_{k},\hat{y}_{k},\sigma_{k}^{2}
comments.explanation: The derivation of the BIV loss assumes sigma_k^2 is provided, but the formulation implicitly assumes sigma_k^2 > 0. If the variance estimation process produces sigma_k^2 = 0, the weight w_k = 1/(sigma_k^2 + xi) is well-defined only if xi > 0. The paper should clarify the domain of sigma_k^2 and the necessity of xi > 0 to ensure the weights remain finite and the loss function does not become singular.

coarse__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Coarse (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
coarse__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback:
The paper proposes Inverse-Variance Reinforcement Learning (IV-RL), a framework that combines variance ensembles and Batch Inverse-Variance weighting to mitigate noisy supervision in model-free deep reinforcement learning. The authors provide a theoretical decomposition of target variance and demonstrate improved sample efficiency on standard discrete and continuous control benchmarks. While the motivation is relevant and the empirical results are promising, the paper lacks critical validation of its uncertainty estimates and practical feasibility analysis.

The paper introduces a clear theoretical framework for decomposing target variance into epistemic and aleatoric components, and applies it effectively to DQN and SAC. The empirical results consistently outperform strong baselines like SUNRISE and BootstrapDQN across multiple environments. The work addresses a relevant gap in leveraging uncertainty for stable value estimation rather than solely for exploration.

Overview issues:

- Theoretical decomposition of target variance overlooks target network bias: Equation 9 decomposes the target variance into predictive uncertainty from the Q-network and stochasticity from the environment and policy. This derivation assumes that the target network provides an unbiased estimate of the value function, or that its estimation error is purely aleatoric. In off-policy algorithms like DQN and SAC, the target network is a slowly updated copy of the online network, introducing a systematic bias and a time-varying variance that the current formulation does not capture. Treating the target network's variance as static or purely epistemic ignores the fact that the regression target shifts during training. This matters because the method down-weights samples based on this variance; if the variance estimate misses the target network's contribution, the weighting will be misaligned with the actual TD error. The authors should extend Equation 9 to account for the target network's update frequency and parameter drift, or empirically demonstrate that this bias is negligible in their experimental setup.

- Lack of uncertainty calibration analysis leaves the core claim unsupported: The paper proposes using variance estimates to weight TD targets, yet it never validates whether these estimates actually correlate with the true error in the value function. Section 4 presents performance curves and ablation studies, but omits any calibration analysis, such as plotting estimated variance against squared TD errors or visualizing how uncertainty maps to exploration trajectories. Without this evidence, the claim that the method successfully distinguishes between reducible epistemic uncertainty and irreducible aleatoric noise remains unsubstantiated. Readers will wonder if the variance networks are merely learning to predict large TD errors as a side effect of the loss attenuation term, rather than providing meaningful uncertainty information. The revision must include a calibration analysis, such as reliability diagrams or correlation metrics between predicted variance and actual target errors, to substantiate the theoretical motivation.

- Computational overhead and practical feasibility for claimed robotics applications: The introduction and conclusion heavily emphasize applying IV-RL to robotics and real-world control tasks where sample efficiency is paramount. However, the method requires training variance ensembles of size N=5 alongside variance networks, which multiplies memory usage and inference time. The main text only briefly mentions that computation time is discussed in Appendix D, and the paper lacks a quantitative discussion of the computational trade-offs. For physical systems or sim-to-real transfer, the overhead of five parallel critics and additional variance outputs could negate the gains from faster convergence. The authors need to explicitly quantify the compute cost per training step and discuss whether the sample efficiency gains justify the increased hardware requirements. A comparison of wall-clock time to sample efficiency would clarify the practical utility of the approach.

- Integration with SAC's min-Q trick and off-policy stability: Section 3.2 describes applying inverse-variance weighting to the actor-critic update, but it does not address how IV-SAC interacts with the standard Soft Actor-Critic architecture. SAC relies on two independent critics and takes the minimum of their Q-values to mitigate value overestimation. Equation 10 applies the BIV loss to a single critic's target, leaving it unclear how the weighting scheme handles the min-Q operation. If both critics are weighted independently, the resulting policy gradients could become unstable or contradict the pessimistic bias that makes SAC stable. Furthermore, the stability of inverse-variance weighting under high TD-error regimes is not analyzed. The authors should clarify the architectural modifications required for SAC, explain how the min-Q trick is preserved, and provide a stability analysis or empirical evidence showing that the critic updates remain well-behaved.

- Mini-batch normalization in BIV may not capture global heteroscedasticity in DRL: The Batch Inverse-Variance weighting scheme normalizes weights within each mini-batch to control the effective learning rate. While this offers stability to the absolute scale of variance estimates, it assumes that the relative variance structure within a batch reflects the global heteroscedasticity of the task. In DRL, replay buffers contain samples from diverse states and time steps, and the variance distribution can change rapidly as the policy improves. Normalizing locally means that a batch with uniformly high variance will receive equal weighting, even if those samples are far from the optimal policy or contain high environmental noise. This could lead to inefficient learning or policy collapse in regions where uncertainty is genuinely high. The authors should discuss the implications of local normalization in non-stationary RL settings and consider whether a global or moving-average normalization scheme would better align with the theoretical goal of down-weighting uncertain targets.

- Concrete parametric example for target variance decomposition: The theoretical derivation in Equation 9 splits target variance into predictive uncertainty and environmental stochasticity, but the paper never computes these quantities for a simple, fully-specified model. Without a worked example, readers cannot verify whether the decomposition behaves as expected in regimes where one component clearly dominates. A natural choice would be a linear quadratic regulator or a tabular MDP with known transition dynamics and reward noise. Computing the analytical variance components for this model and comparing them to the neural network estimates would ground the theoretical claims in concrete intuition. This demonstration would also clarify how the discount factor scales the predictive variance relative to the aleatoric noise.

- Consistent sample efficiency metric across discrete and continuous tasks: The experimental evaluation uses different metrics to quantify sample efficiency for DQN and SAC, which obscures direct comparison. Table 1 reports the median episodes to reach a solved score for DQN, while Table 2 reports average returns at fixed step counts for SAC. Readers cannot easily assess whether the reported gains in one setting translate to the other or how the methods scale across different time horizons. The revision should adopt a unified metric, such as steps-to-convergence or a normalized learning curve, for both algorithm families. Plotting these curves with confidence intervals would allow a direct visual comparison of the learning speed across all environments and baselines.

- Verification that BIV recovers standard L2 loss under uniform variance: The paper claims that Batch Inverse-Variance weighting is robust to poorly calibrated variance estimates, but it never formally demonstrates that the method behaves correctly when variance estimates are uninformative. If all estimated variances are identical, the BIV loss should mathematically collapse to the standard L2 loss used in conventional DRL. The authors should derive this reduction explicitly and verify it empirically by running an ablation where variance networks output constant values. Showing this baseline behavior establishes that the method does not introduce unintended optimization bias when the uncertainty signals are flat.

- Discussion of variance estimation challenges in high-dimensional state spaces: The method requires predicting a variance parameter for every state-action pair in high-dimensional continuous control tasks, yet the paper provides no analysis of how this scales with input dimensionality. Predicting heteroscedastic noise in deep networks is known to suffer from instability and variance collapse, especially when the state space is large and the policy explores diverse regions. The authors should discuss how they initialize the variance networks, whether they use any regularization to prevent variance from collapsing to zero, and how the method copes with the curse of dimensionality when estimating conditional noise. Including these implementation details in the main text would clarify the practical feasibility of the approach beyond the reported compute overhead.

Recommendation: major revision. The paper presents a methodologically sound idea but lacks critical validation of its core assumptions and practical feasibility. The absence of uncertainty calibration analysis, combined with unresolved questions about SAC integration and computational overhead, prevents a confident assessment of the claims. Addressing these gaps requires substantial additional experimentation and theoretical clarification.

Revision targets:

- Add a calibration analysis correlating predicted variance with actual TD errors, including reliability plots and trajectory visualizations.

- Clarify the architectural implementation for SAC, specifically how the min-Q trick and dual-critic setup interact with the BIV weighting scheme.

- Quantify the computational trade-offs by reporting wall-clock training times and memory usage relative to sample efficiency gains.

- Extend the variance decomposition in Equation 9 to account for target network bias and parameter drift, or provide empirical evidence that this effect is negligible.

comments.title: Incorrect formulation of predictive variance for Gaussian mixtures
comments.quote: The predictive variance is given by a Gaussian mixture over the variance predictions of each network in the ensemble.
comments.explanation: This statement conflates a distribution with a scalar quantity and omits the variance of the means. For a Gaussian mixture $p(y|x) = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^N \mathcal{N}(y; \mu_i, \sigma_i^2)$, the predictive variance follows the law of total variance: $\text{Var}(y|x) = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^N \sigma_i^2 + \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^N (\mu_i - \bar{\mu})^2$. The original phrasing implies $\text{Var}(y|x) \approx \frac{1}{N}\sum \sigma_i^2$, which ignores epistemic uncertainty captured by mean disagreement. This is a fundamental error in defining the ensemble's uncertainty quantification. Rewrite the sentence to explicitly state that the predictive distribution is modeled as a Gaussian mixture and that the variance includes both aleatoric and epistemic components.

comments.title: Contradiction between mixture variance formula and calibration claim
comments.quote: in the final variance estimation because the mixture of Gaussians dampens single very high variances
comments.explanation: The claim that a Gaussian mixture 'dampens single very high variances' directly contradicts the law of total variance. If one network predicts a very high variance $\sigma_k^2 \gg \sigma_{j \neq k}^2$, the mixture variance $\frac{1}{N}\sum \sigma_i^2$ will be dominated by that high value, increasing rather than dampening the overall uncertainty estimate. The empirical calibration improvement in deep ensembles typically stems from averaging the predictive distributions rather than a mathematical damping property of the mixture itself. The authors should clarify whether they refer to averaging the variance predictions or the full mixture variance, and correct the mechanistic explanation.

comments.title: UCB bonus incorrectly uses variance instead of standard deviation
comments.quote: As the variance in UCB is added to $Q$-values, it must be calibrated: we evaluate it with sampled ensembles.
comments.explanation: The statement that 'variance in UCB is added to $Q$-values' is dimensionally inconsistent. Upper Confidence Bound methods construct exploration bonuses from confidence intervals, which scale with the standard deviation (the square root of variance), not the variance itself. Adding raw variance would alter the units of the bonus and make the exploration signal highly sensitive to the reward scale. The authors should correct this to state that the standard deviation is added to the Q-values. Additionally, the phrase 'we evaluate it with sampled ensembles' is ambiguous. The authors likely mean that ensembles are used to estimate the predictive variance or standard deviation during training, not to calibrate it.

comments.title: PPO mischaracterized as a TD Q-learning method
comments.quote: e updates. In methods such as DQN (Mnih et al., 2013), PPO (Schulman et al., 2017) and SAC (Haarnoja et al., 2018), a neural network is trained to predict the  $Q$ -value of a given state-action pair  $Q^{\pi}(s,a)^{4}$  by minimizing the error between the target  $T(s,a)$  and its prediction  $\hat{Q}(s,
comments.explanation: PPO does not fit the described update mechanism. PPO is an on-policy policy gradient algorithm that optimizes a clipped surrogate objective to directly update the policy parameters. It typically relies on a separate value network to estimate $V(s)$ for advantage calculation, rather than training a Q-network via an L2 TD loss. Including PPO in this list obscures the distinction between value-based/TD methods and policy gradient methods. Remove PPO from this list or clarify that the discussion applies specifically to TD-based algorithms.

comments.title: Notation inconsistency between Q-bar and Q-hat
comments.quote: 4.  $\bar{Q}$  is a prediction from a var-network  $p_{\hat{Q}}(\hat{Q} |s',a')$
comments.explanation: The bullet introduces the variable as $\bar{Q}$ but immediately assigns it the distribution $p_{\hat{Q}}(\hat{Q} |s',a')$. This switch within a single clause creates ambiguity about whether $\bar{Q}$ and $\hat{Q}$ represent the same quantity or distinct network outputs. Additionally, the term 'var-network' is non-standard without prior definition; typically, a value network predicts the mean Q-value while a separate head or network predicts variance. To resolve this, the authors should unify the symbol (e.g., use $\hat{Q}$ consistently) and clarify the network's output structure.

comments.title: Conditional phrasing misrepresents the generative model
comments.explanation: The generative model description incorrectly conditions the sampling steps on the stochasticity of the components. In reinforcement learning, deterministic rewards, dynamics, and policies are simply degenerate probability distributions. A correct generative model should unconditionally specify sampling from these distributions (or point masses), as the mathematical formulation of the Bellman target and its variance relies on the distributions themselves, not on conditional branches. Rewrite the bullet points to unconditionally state: 'sample $r \sim p_R(r|s,a)$', 'sample $s' \sim p_{S'}(s'|s,a)$', and 'sample $a' \sim \pi(a'|s')$' because this accurately reflects that deterministic components are just degenerate distributions within the same probabilistic framework.

comments.title: Omission of discount factor and combination rule
comments.quote: 5.  $T$  is deterministically generated from  $r$  and  $\bar{Q}$  by equation (4).
comments.explanation: The description of the target generation omits the discount factor $\gamma$ and the specific algebraic combination of $r$ and $\bar{Q}$. A valid Bellman target must incorporate the discount factor (e.g., $T = r + \gamma \bar{Q}$ or similar). Without explicitly stating how $r$ and $\bar{Q}$ are combined, or referencing the exact form of equation (4) in the text, readers cannot verify the derivation of the target's distribution or its heteroscedasticity claim. The proof of heteroscedasticity later in the paragraph depends entirely on this combination rule. Add the explicit combination formula after 'by equation (4)' to clarify the target construction and enable verification of the subsequent variance claims.

comments.title: Overgeneralization of algorithmic applicability
comments.quote: We applied IV-RL to DQN and SAC, but it can be adapted to any model-free algorithm.
comments.explanation: The conclusion asserts that IV-RL can be adapted to any model-free algorithm, yet the technical sections only demonstrate its application to DQN and SAC. The paper does not address how the inverse-variance weighting interacts with critical off-policy mechanisms like SAC's min-Q trick, which is essential for preventing value overestimation in actor-critic methods. Without clarifying how the weighting scheme preserves the pessimistic bias required for stable off-policy learning, this broad claim remains unsupported. The authors should restrict this statement to the algorithms tested or provide a brief discussion on the necessary modifications for other model-free families.

comments.title: Overstated feasibility for real-world robotics deployment
comments.quote: We thus believe that IV-RL is a significant step to enable the application of DRL to real-world problems such as robotics, where sample efficiency is the main challenge.
comments.explanation: The conclusion claims that IV-RL is a significant step for real-world robotics applications, but the method relies on training variance ensembles of size N=5 alongside dedicated variance networks. This architecture substantially increases memory footprint and inference latency compared to standard baselines. In physical robotics or sim-to-real transfer, such computational overhead can negate sample efficiency gains and complicate deployment on resource-constrained edge hardware. The authors should temper this claim by explicitly acknowledging the compute-memory trade-offs and clarifying the specific conditions under which the sample efficiency benefits outweigh the hardware costs for real-world control tasks.

comments.title: Formal results lack theoretical justification for noise analysis claim
comments.quote: A thorough analysis of the sources of noise that contribute to errors in the target motivates the use of a combination of Batch Inverse Variance (BIV) weighting and variance ensembles to estimate the variance of the target and down weight the uncertain samples in two complementary ways. Our results show that these two components are beneficial and that their combination significantly improves the state of the art in terms of learning efficiency, in particular for control tasks.
comments.explanation: The provided formal results section (Section 2.1) only defines the BIV loss function and the effective batch size calculation. It does not contain any mathematical derivation, theoretical analysis, or proof regarding the sources of noise in the target or the justification for combining BIV with variance ensembles. The conclusion asserts that a 'thorough analysis' motivates this design choice, but the formal section merely presents the loss formulation and a heuristic for tuning $\xi$. This creates a mismatch between the theoretical claims in the discussion and the descriptive content of the formal results. Additionally, the claim that the combination 'significantly improves the state of the art' is an empirical finding that belongs in the experimental section, not a formal result. To resolve this, the authors should either include the noise analysis and theoretical motivation in the formal results section or temper the conclusion's claims to reflect that the combination is empirically validated and based on established heteroscedastic regression principles rather than a novel theoretical analysis presented here.

comments.title: Misattribution of SUNRISE to Lee et al. (2021)
comments.quote:
ty estimation.

Closer to our work, *Lee et al. (2021)* propose SUNRISE, in which each sample of the Bellman backup in the TD update step is weighted to lower the importance of the targets which have a high standard deviation. T
comments.explanation: The SUNRISE framework is actually authored by Wang, Vahdat, and Paull (2021), not Lee et al. (2021). This misattribution should be corrected to accurately credit the prior work. Please update the citation to the correct reference.

comments.title: Notation mismatch in SUNRISE weight formula
comments.quote: The weights $w(s^{\prime},a^{\prime})$ are computed based on a sigmoid of the negative standard deviation $\hat{Q}_{\mathrm{std}}(s^{\prime},a^{\prime})$ scaled by a temperature hyperparameter $T$, and then offset such that they are between 0.5 and 1: $w(s,a)=\sigma(-\hat{Q}_{\mathrm{std}}(s^{\prime},a^{\prime})*T)+0.5$.
comments.explanation: The left-hand side defines the weight as $w(s,a)$, but the right-hand side depends on the next state-action pair $(s',a')$. This index mismatch is confusing. Rewrite the equation to consistently use $(s',a')$ on both sides, i.e., $w(s',a')=\sigma(-\hat{Q}_{\mathrm{std}}(s',a')\cdot T)+0.5$, to match the preceding textual description.

comments.title: Imprecise description of variance merging and Ovadia et al. citation
comments.quote: We merge the predicted variances of several variance networks through a mixture of Gaussians, which has been shown to be a reliable method to capture predictive uncertainty *(Ovadia et al., 2019)*.
comments.explanation: Mixture of Gaussians (MoG) aggregates predictive distributions, not variances directly. The correct procedure is to treat each network's output as a Gaussian distribution, form a MoG over these distributions, and then compute the resulting mixture variance. Additionally, Ovadia et al. (2019) evaluates uncertainty estimation methods but does not propose MoG for variance merging. Clarify the aggregation procedure and cite a source that specifically discusses MoG for ensemble variance estimation, or remove the Ovadia citation from this specific claim.

comments.title: Conceptual inaccuracy in comparing sample masking to MC dropout
comments.quote: ning. This method, used by *Clements et al. (2020)* and *Lee et al. (2021)*, has the same principle as single network Monte-Carlo dropout *(Gal and Ghahramani, 2016)*. As
comments.explanation: Equating sample masking to MC dropout conflates two distinct sources of stochasticity. MC dropout introduces randomness directly into the network's function approximation by randomly zeroing activations or weights. In contrast, applying a random Bernoulli mask to training samples is a form of data subsampling that decorrelates optimization trajectories by exposing each network to a different subset of the dataset. Rewrite the sentence to clarify that sample masking operates on the dataset rather than network parameters, making the theoretical comparison inaccurate.

comments.title: Mischaracterization of Randomized Prior Functions' role in variance
comments.quote: this problem, Randomized Prior Functions (RPFs) enforce a prior in the variance by pairing each network with a fixed, untrained network which adds its predictions to the output *(Osband et al., 2019)*. RPFs ensure a high variance at regions of the input space which are not well explored, and a lower variance when the trained networks have learned to compensate for their respective prior and converge to the same output. The scale
comments.explanation: The claim that RPFs 'enforce a prior in the variance' misrepresents the mechanism. In the standard formulation, the fixed prior network is added to the mean output of each trained network to provide a non-zero baseline prediction in unexplored regions, thereby encouraging exploration. The ensemble variance is computed exclusively over the trained networks' outputs and reflects their disagreement. The prior does not mathematically guarantee high variance; rather, high variance naturally persists in unexplored regions because the trained networks have not yet converged to the same function. Rewrite the quoted passage to clarify that RPFs shift the mean prediction rather than directly enforcing variance.

comments.title: Missing positivity constraint on predicted variance
comments.quote: $\mathcal{L}_{LA}(D,\theta)=\sum_{k=0}^{K}\frac{1}{K}\frac{\left(\mu_{\theta}(x_{k})-y(x_{k})\right)^{2}}{\sigma_{\theta}^{2}(x_{k})}+\ln\sigma_{\theta}^{2}(x_{k})$ (3)
comments.explanation: The loss function involves division by $\sigma_{\theta}^{2}(x_{k})$ and a logarithm $\ln\sigma_{\theta}^{2}(x_{k})$, both of which require the predicted variance to be strictly positive. The text does not specify how the network parameterizes $\sigma_{\theta}^{2}(x)$ to guarantee this condition (e.g., via an exponential parameterization $\sigma^2 = \exp(s(x))$ or a softplus activation). Without such a constraint, the loss is undefined for non-positive outputs, and gradient descent may encounter singularities. Add a specification of the output activation or parameterization that enforces $\sigma_{\theta}^{2}(x) > 0$ for all $x$.

comments.title: Algorithmic details deferred to appendix hinder readability
comments.quote: We have adapted IV-RL to DQN and SAC to produce IV-DQN and IV-SAC. Comprehensive pseudo-code, as well as implementation details for each algorithm, can be found in appendix A.
comments.explanation: The section titled '3.3 Algorithms' contains no mathematical formulations, update rules, or pseudo-code. It merely states that adaptations exist and points the reader to Appendix A. This forces readers to leave the main text to understand how the inverse-variance weighting integrates with the specific critic and actor updates of DQN and SAC. Given that the paper's core contribution is the adaptation of uncertainty estimation to these algorithms, the key modifications to the target computation and loss functions should be summarized directly in Section 3.3. Rewrite this section to include the modified Bellman backup equations and the BIV loss formulation for both IV-DQN and IV-SAC, keeping only hyperparameter tuning details in the appendix.

comments.title: Textual description of SunriseSAC performance contradicts Table 2
comments.quote: Except in Ant, the SUNRISE weights do not seem to lead to consistently better results.
comments.explanation: This claim directly conflicts with the numerical results presented in Table 2. When comparing SunriseSAC to the EnsembleSAC baseline, Sunrise actually underperforms in HalfCheetah at 100k steps (3879 vs 3938) and in Hopper at 200k steps (2572 vs 2652). Conversely, in Ant, Sunrise outperforms EnsembleSAC at 200k steps (1462 vs 981) and is only marginally lower at 100k steps (834 vs 852). The statement should be corrected to accurately reflect the environments where SunriseSAC fails to consistently beat the ensemble baseline, which are primarily HalfCheetah and Hopper depending on the evaluation step count.

comments.title: Mismatch between Figure 5 text description and caption
comments.quote: Similarly to IV-DQN, we can separate the contribution from BIV and loss attenuation, as shown in the two first plots of figure 5. While both BIV and var-ensembles alone have a significant impact in Walker, it's only their combination that brings the most improvement in HalfCheetah.
comments.explanation: The main text claims that the first two plots of Figure 5 demonstrate the separation of BIV and loss attenuation contributions. However, the figure caption explicitly states that these plots show the 'Impact of using different uncertainty estimation methods.' This discrepancy creates ambiguity about what the ablation actually measures and whether the visual evidence supports the textual claim. The authors need to ensure the figure caption and main text description are aligned. Update the figure caption to explicitly mention the ablation of BIV and loss attenuation, or revise the main text to match the caption's description of uncertainty estimation methods.

coarse__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Coarse (deepseek-v4-flash)
coarse__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback:
The paper proposes a novel framework (IV-RL) that combines Batch Inverse Variance weighting with variance ensembles to mitigate the impact of noisy supervision in model-free deep RL. The core idea—decomposing target uncertainty into prediction variance and environment/policy variance and weighting accordingly—is well-motivated and the empirical results show consistent sample efficiency gains across discrete and continuous control tasks. The paper is clearly written and includes useful ablations. However, several methodological concerns regarding the calibration and stability of variance estimates, the handling of non-stationary targets, and the computational cost relative to gains need to be addressed before the contribution is fully convincing.

Overview issues:

- Variance estimates may be biased or high-variance, undermining the weighting scheme: The paper assumes that the variance estimates from var-ensembles (Section 2.2.3) are unbiased and can be plugged directly into the BIV loss (Equation 10). However, in off-policy RL with function approximation, variance predictions from negative log-likelihood trained networks are known to be miscalibrated, especially early in training or with limited data (as noted in the literature context). The paper does not provide calibration plots or any diagnostic showing that the variance estimates are reliable. If the variances are noisy or biased, the inverse-variance weighting could actually harm learning by giving high weight to samples with spuriously low variance. The authors should include calibration analysis (e.g., reliability diagrams or variance over training) and discuss how the ξ hyperparameter mitigates this risk beyond the minimal EBS heuristic.

- Non-stationarity of the target distribution is not addressed: The derivation of the target variance in Equation 9 assumes a fixed policy and environment. However, in deep RL, the policy changes rapidly during training, which shifts the distribution of Q-values and thus the variance of the target. The paper acknowledges that the target changes (Section 3.1.2) but does not discuss how the variance estimates remain valid under non-stationarity. The var-ensemble is trained on data from a replay buffer that contains stale transitions, so the variance predictions may reflect old Q-values rather than current ones. This could lead to weighting that is systematically misaligned with the true uncertainty. The authors should either provide a mechanism to adapt variance estimates to policy changes (e.g., using importance weights or online updates) or empirically demonstrate that the weighting remains beneficial despite this mismatch.

- Inverse-variance weighting may discard informative high-variance updates in sparse-reward settings: The paper's core mechanism down-weights samples with high target variance. In sparse-reward environments, the few transitions that yield a reward may have high variance because the Q-values are uncertain, yet these are precisely the most informative samples for learning. The results on MountainCar (Figure 2) show no improvement over baselines, which the authors attribute to the exploration challenge. But the problem is more fundamental: in any environment where reward is rare, the high-variance samples are the ones that carry the most information, and down-weighting them could slow learning. The paper should discuss this trade-off explicitly and provide analysis (e.g., by showing the effective weight on reward-yielding transitions) to assure readers that the method does not inadvertently discard critical updates.

- Computational overhead of variance ensembles is not adequately justified: The method uses an ensemble of 5 variance networks for both the Q-network and the target network, plus a separate ensemble for exploration. This multiplies the computational cost by at least 5-10x compared to a single-network baseline. The paper mentions computation time in Appendix D but does not report wall-clock time or FLOPs in the main text. For a method that claims to improve sample efficiency, the practical utility depends on whether the sample efficiency gains outweigh the computational cost. If IV-SAC requires 5x more computation per step, then a baseline that runs for 5x more steps might achieve similar or better results. The authors should report wall-clock time to reach a given performance level and compare against baselines that are allowed equivalent compute (e.g., a single-network SAC run for 5x more steps).

- The actor weighting in IV-SAC lacks theoretical justification: Section 3.2 proposes using inverse-variance weighting for the actor update (Equation 13), treating the Q-value as a random variable with known variance. However, the actor objective is to maximize the expected Q-value, and the Bayesian estimator in Equation 13 is derived under the assumption that the Q-values are independent across samples. In practice, the Q-values in a mini-batch are correlated because they come from the same replay buffer and the same network. Moreover, the variance used for weighting is the predictive variance of the critic, not the variance of the Q-value estimate for the actor's policy. The paper does not justify why weighting by the critic's variance improves the actor's gradient. This is a significant gap because the actor update is a key component of SAC. The authors should either provide a theoretical rationale or remove this component and show that the method still works with only the critic weighting.

- No worked example of variance decomposition: The paper decomposes the target variance into two components in Equation 9 but never computes these components for a concrete, fully-specified model. A natural worked example would be to take a simple gridworld or a linear-quadratic regulator (LQR) environment with known transition and reward noise, compute the two variance terms analytically, and verify that the var-ensemble and loss-attenuation estimates recover them. Without this, readers cannot calibrate their intuition about when each component matters or whether the decomposition is identifiable in practice. The authors should add a short section or appendix computing Equation 9 for a toy MDP (e.g., a two-state chain with stochastic rewards) and showing that the two estimation methods correctly separate the sources.

- Missing demonstration that weighting is not harming exploration: The paper shows that IV-DQN does not improve on MountainCar, an exploration-heavy environment, but does not investigate why. A deeper concern is whether inverse-variance weighting systematically discards the very transitions that are most informative for exploration — those with high uncertainty. The paper should include an analysis on a sparse-reward environment (e.g., a harder exploration task like DeepSea from BSuite) that tracks the weights assigned to reward-yielding versus non-reward transitions. If high-variance reward transitions are down-weighted, the method could actually hurt exploration in settings where it matters most. The authors should show that the weighting does not suppress the learning signal from rare rewards, or discuss a modification to prevent this.

- No practical guidance on setting the ξ hyperparameter: The paper introduces ξ as a key hyperparameter that controls the effective mini-batch size and robustness to miscalibrated variances, but provides almost no guidance on how to set it in practice. The minimal-EBS heuristic is mentioned but not evaluated: there is no experiment showing how performance varies with the target EBS ratio, nor any recommendation for a default value. Since ξ directly determines how aggressively uncertain samples are down-weighted, practitioners have no basis for choosing it. The authors should include a sensitivity analysis on ξ (or the EBS ratio) across at least two environments, and suggest a default value or tuning procedure.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Provide calibration analysis (e.g., reliability diagrams or variance over training) for the variance estimates from var-ensembles, and discuss how the ξ hyperparameter mitigates miscalibration.

- Address the non-stationarity issue: either adapt variance estimates to policy changes or provide empirical evidence that the weighting remains beneficial despite stale variances.

- Analyze the trade-off in sparse-reward settings: show that informative high-variance samples (e.g., reward-yielding transitions) are not excessively down-weighted.

- Report wall-clock time or FLOPs to reach a given performance level and compare against baselines with equivalent compute budget.

- Justify the actor weighting theoretically or remove it and demonstrate that the method still works with only the critic weighting.

comments.title: Gaussian noise assumption for var-network is unjustified
comments.quote: Assuming a zero mean and normally distributed noise, this underlying stochasticity of the generating process is well-captured by a var-network with a loss attenuation using the negative log-likelihood formulation described in equation (3).
comments.explanation: The Gaussian assumption is not justified for RL targets, which can be non-Gaussian. The paper does not provide empirical evidence (e.g., residual analysis) that the noise is approximately Gaussian. The authors should either justify the assumption (e.g., by citing the central limit theorem for returns) or discuss limitations. A sentence acknowledging that the Gaussian assumption is a modeling choice and may not hold in all environments, and that the var-network may still provide useful variance estimates even if the true distribution is not Gaussian, would be appropriate.

comments.title: Bayesian estimator derivation for actor weighting is incomplete
comments.quote:
If we instead consider the critic’s estimation $\hat{Q}(s_{k},a_{k})$ as a random variable sampled from $p_{\hat{Q}}(\hat{Q}|s,a)$, with mean $\mu_{\hat{Q}}(s_{k},a_{k})$ and variance $\sigma^{2}_{\hat{Q}}(s_{k},a_{k})$, we can instead infer the expected value in equation (11) using Bayesian estimation *(Murphy, 2012)*:

$\left(\sum_{k=0}^{K}1/\sigma^{2}_{\hat{Q}}\left(s_{k},a_{k}\right)\right)^{-1}\sum_{k=0}^{K}\frac{1}{\sigma^{2}_{\hat{Q}}(s_{k},a_{k})}\mu_{\hat{Q}}(s_{k},a_{k})$
comments.explanation: The derivation from (11) to (13) is missing steps. The paper does not specify the prior distribution or the likelihood model, and the reference to Murphy (2012) is too vague to justify the specific formula. The estimator in (13) is the posterior mean for independent Gaussian observations with known variances under a flat prior, but the paper does not state these assumptions or verify they hold. The claim that (13) follows from Bayesian estimation is therefore unsupported. Rewrite the paragraph to include a clear derivation: state that each $\hat{Q}(s_k,a_k)$ is assumed to be an independent draw from $\mathcal{N}(\mu_{\hat{Q}}(s_k,a_k), \sigma^2_{\hat{Q}}(s_k,a_k))$, adopt an improper uniform prior on the common mean $\mathbb{E}[Q(s,a)]$, and then compute the posterior mean, which yields (13). Alternatively, remove the Bayesian justification and present (13) as a heuristic weighted average.

comments.title: Actor weighting lacks theoretical justification
comments.quote: The normalized inverse-variance weights are a direct fit with the BIV loss in equation 1: we therefore also use BIV to train the actor. As the target and the $Q$-networks have the same structure, $\sigma^{2}_{\hat{Q}}(s_{k},a_{k})$ can be estimated using the same var-ensembles used in section 3.1.2.
comments.explanation: The paper claims that using inverse-variance weighting for the actor update is a 'direct fit' with the BIV loss, but provides no derivation or justification. The BIV loss (Equation 1) is a weighted regression loss for the critic, whereas the actor's objective is to maximize the expected Q-value. Weighting the actor's gradient by $1/\sigma^2_{\hat{Q}}$ changes the gradient direction in an unsubstantiated way. The paper should either derive the weighted gradient as the gradient of a well-defined objective (e.g., a lower bound on the expected Q-value) or provide empirical evidence that this weighting improves performance. Without such justification, the claim that BIV is used to train the actor is unsupported.

comments.title: Independence assumption for actor weighting is not justified
comments.quote: If we instead consider the critic’s estimation $\hat{Q}(s_{k},a_{k})$ as a random variable sampled from $p_{\hat{Q}}(\hat{Q}|s,a)$, with mean $\mu_{\hat{Q}}(s_{k},a_{k})$ and variance $\sigma^{2}_{\hat{Q}}(s_{k},a_{k})$, we can instead infer the expected value in equation (11) using Bayesian estimation *(Murphy, 2012)*:
comments.explanation: The Bayesian estimator in (13) assumes independence of the $\hat{Q}(s_k,a_k)$ values, but in practice these values are correlated because they are computed by the same critic network (or ensemble) and share underlying parameters. The paper does not address this correlation or justify why it can be ignored. The estimator for correlated observations would involve the full covariance matrix, which is not provided. The authors should either argue that the correlation is negligible (e.g., due to the ensemble or dropout providing independent samples) or modify the estimator to account for it.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated calibration claim for variance ensembles
comments.quote: Similarly to sampled ensembles, variance ensembles suffer from underestimated early epistemic variance estimation. However, compared to variance networks, they seem empirically less prone to calibration issues (1) in the final variance estimation because the mixture of Gaussians dampens single very high variances, and (2) in the learning process because even if one network does not learn correctly, the others will.
comments.explanation: The claim that variance ensembles are 'empirically less prone to calibration issues' is presented as a fact but is not supported by any citation or experiment in this section. The reasoning given — that a mixture of Gaussians dampens high variances and that multiple networks compensate for individual failures — is heuristic and does not guarantee better calibration. Calibration is a specific property (e.g., the predicted variance matches the empirical MSE), and it is not obvious that averaging variances from an ensemble improves calibration over a single variance network. The paper should either cite a study that directly compares calibration of variance ensembles vs. variance networks, or provide its own empirical evidence (e.g., calibration plots) before making this claim. Without such support, the statement is speculative and could mislead readers about the reliability of the variance estimates used in the core IV-RL method.

comments.title: Missing regularization for variance in BIV term
comments.quote:
=\mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{BIV}}(D,\theta)+\lambda\mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{LA}}(D,\theta)$
$=\sum_{k=0}^{K}\left[\left(\sum_{j=0}^{K}\frac{1}{\gamma^{2}\sigma_{\bar{Q},j}^{2}+\xi}\right)^{-1}\frac{\left(\mu_{\bar{Q}_{\theta}}(s_{k},a_{k})-T(s_{k},a_{k})\right)^{2}}{\gamma^{2}\sigma_{\bar{Q},k}^{2}+\xi}\right.$
$\left.+\frac{\lambda}{K}\left(\frac{\left(\mu_{\bar{Q}_{\theta}}(s_{k},a_{k})-T(s_{k},a_{k})\right)^{2}}{\sigma_{\bar{Q}_{\theta}}^{2}(s_{k},a_{k})}+\ln\sigma_{\bar{Q}_{\theta}}^{2}(s_{k},a_{k})\right)\right]$ (10)
comments.explanation: The BIV loss term in Equation (10) does not include a regularization term for the variance $\sigma_{\bar{Q},k}^2$ (e.g., a log term to prevent it from collapsing to zero). Without such regularization, the optimization could drive $\sigma_{\bar{Q},k}^2$ to zero to minimize the weighted squared error, which would make the loss degenerate. The LA term includes $\ln \sigma_{\bar{Q}_{\theta}}^2$ to prevent this, but the BIV term lacks an analogous mechanism. Add a regularization term (e.g., $\ln(\gamma^2 \sigma_{\bar{Q},k}^2 + \xi)$) to the BIV loss to stabilize training.

comments.title: Section 3.3 lacks substantive content
