comments.quote: We have adapted IV-RL to DQN and SAC to produce IV-DQN and IV-SAC. Comprehensive pseudo-code, as well as implementation details for each algorithm, can be found in appendix A.
comments.explanation: This section is only two sentences long and contains no technical content—no equations, no algorithmic description, no justification of how IV-RL is adapted to DQN and SAC. The reader is immediately redirected to an appendix without any high-level summary of the adaptations. This is a structural failure: Section 3.3 should at minimum state the key modifications (e.g., how the variance weighting is applied to the DQN loss or the SAC actor/critic losses) and note any differences from the generic IV-RL formulation. Without this, the section is essentially a placeholder.

comments.title: MountainCar solved score threshold is ambiguous
comments.quote: |   | LunarLander (200) | Cartpole-Noise (750) | MountainCar (-150)  |
comments.explanation: The column header for MountainCar reads 'MountainCar (-150)', which is ambiguous. The value -150 is listed in parentheses, but it is not clear whether this is the solved-score threshold or the average return at which the environment is considered solved. For LunarLander and Cartpole-Noise, the thresholds 200 and 750 are clearly positive scores. For MountainCar, the threshold -150 is a negative return, which is unusual because the environment's default solved condition is often defined as reaching the flag (return >= -110 or similar). The paper should explicitly state the solved-score threshold for MountainCar and justify why -150 was chosen.

comments.title: Cartpole-Noise column shows 'max' for DQN 75th percentile
comments.explanation: The entry for DQN in the Cartpole-Noise column shows 'max' for the 75th percentile. This is ambiguous: does it mean that the 75th percentile of episodes to reach the solved score exceeds the maximum number of training episodes (i.e., DQN never reached the solved score in 25% of runs)? Or does it indicate that the value is the maximum observed across runs? The paper should clarify what 'max' represents. If it means that DQN failed to reach the solved score in some runs, then the median and 25th percentile values are conditional on successful runs only, which biases the comparison.

comments.title: Table 2 reports standard error but text says standard deviation
comments.quote: The results show the mean and standard error over 25 runs.
comments.explanation: The table caption states that the reported values are standard errors, but the text in Section 4.2 (and the paper's abstract) typically reports standard deviations for RL results. Standard error is standard deviation divided by sqrt(N), so for 25 seeds the standard error is 5 times smaller than the standard deviation. This makes the confidence intervals appear artificially tight and could mislead readers about the variability of the results. The authors should clarify whether the table reports standard deviation or standard error, and if it is standard error, they should also report the standard deviation or note the sample size explicitly so readers can convert.

comments.title: Claim of 'significant improvements' lacks statistical test
comments.quote: In comparison, IV-SAC leads to significant improvements in performance.
comments.explanation: The text claims that IV-SAC leads to 'significant improvements' over baselines, but no statistical significance test (e.g., paired t-test, Mann-Whitney U, or confidence interval overlap analysis) is reported. With 25 seeds, one can compute whether the difference in mean returns is statistically significant. For example, in the Ant environment at 200k steps, IV-SAC's mean (1281) is actually lower than SunriseSAC's (1462), yet the text still claims improvement. The authors should either perform a proper significance test and report p-values or confidence intervals, or soften the language to 'numerical improvements' where appropriate.

comments.title: Ant result at 200k steps contradicts claim of improvement
comments.quote: cantly better than the baselines at 200k steps, such as in Ant or Hopper, it clearly is learning faster, which is also reflected by the scores at 100k steps.
comments.explanation: The text acknowledges that IV-SAC is not significantly better at 200k steps in Ant and Hopper, but claims it 'clearly is learning faster' based on 100k-step scores. However, in Ant at 100k steps, IV-SAC (948 ± 46) is only marginally higher than EnsembleSAC (852 ± 27) and SunriseSAC (834 ± 130), and the standard errors overlap substantially. Moreover, at 200k steps, IV-SAC (1281 ± 101) is actually lower than SunriseSAC (1462 ± 154). This pattern does not convincingly demonstrate faster learning; it could be that IV-SAC plateaus earlier. The authors should either provide a statistical test for learning speed (e.g., area under the learning curve) or temper the claim.

comments.title: Overclaiming improvement over state of the art
comments.quote: Our results show that these two components are beneficial and that their combination significantly improves the state of the art in terms of learning efficiency, in particular for control tasks.
comments.explanation: The paper claims that IV-RL 'significantly improves the state of the art' in learning efficiency. However, the results in Section 4 show that IV-DQN does not improve over baselines on all Atari games (e.g., no improvement on MountainCar, and only marginal gains on some games). The improvement is primarily on MuJoCo control tasks with IV-SAC. The claim as stated is too broad and overstates the contribution. Rewrite to specify the settings where improvement is observed, e.g., 'Our results show that these two components are beneficial and that their combination improves learning efficiency on continuous control tasks, while results on discrete-action Atari games are mixed.'

comments.title: Ethics statement carbon footprint calculation error
comments.quote: Total emissions are estimated to be 2160.76 kgCO_{2}eq of which 0 percents were directly offset. This is equivalent to 8730 km driven by an average car, or 1.08 metric tons of burned coal.
comments.explanation: The carbon footprint equivalences are inconsistent. 2160.76 kgCO2eq is 2.16076 metric tons, not 1.08 metric tons of burned coal. The claim '1.08 metric tons of burned coal' appears to be a miscalculation or misstatement. Additionally, the equivalence to '8730 km driven by an average car' seems plausible (assuming ~0.25 kgCO2/km), but the coal figure is off by a factor of 2. The authors should correct the coal equivalence or remove it to avoid misleading readers.

comments.title: Missing hyperparameter details in reproducibility statement
comments.quote: To allow reproducibility of our results, the code, as well as the hyperparameters used to produce each result presented in this paper, are available at https://github.com/montrealrobotics/iv_rl.
comments.explanation: The statement claims that hyperparameters are available at the repository, but the paper's main text and appendix do not list the key hyperparameters (e.g., ξ, ensemble size, learning rates, buffer sizes) in a self-contained table. A reproducibility statement should either include a table of all hyperparameters in the paper or explicitly state that the repository contains a configuration file. Without this, a reader cannot reproduce results without downloading and running the code, which is insufficient for archival reproducibility.

comments.title: Missing random seed information for reproducibility
comments.quote: The environments and implementation we used (OpenAI Gym, BSuite, MBBL) are all publicly accessible, although a Mujoco license is needed to run some of them.
comments.explanation: The reproducibility statement does not mention how random seeds were set or whether the codebase seeds all sources of randomness (e.g., NumPy, PyTorch, environment seeds). Without this information, results may not be exactly reproducible even with the same code and hyperparameters. Add a sentence specifying the seeding strategy (e.g., 'All experiments used fixed seeds for Python, NumPy, PyTorch, and each environment, with seeds listed in the repository.')

comments.title: Mischaracterization of SUNRISE weighting scheme
comments.quote: The weights $w(s^{\prime},a^{\prime})$ are computed based on a sigmoid of the negative standard deviation $\hat{Q}_{\mathrm{std}}(s^{\prime},a^{\prime})$ scaled by a temperature hyperparameter $T$, and then offset such that they are between 0.5 and 1: $w(s,a)=\sigma(-\hat{Q}_{\mathrm{std}}(s^{\prime},a^{\prime})*T)+0.5$.
comments.explanation: The description of the SUNRISE weighting scheme is incorrect. In the original SUNRISE paper (Lee et al., 2021), the weight for a sample is computed as $w(s,a) = \sigma(-\hat{Q}_{\mathrm{std}}(s,a) \cdot T) + 0.5$, where $\hat{Q}_{\mathrm{std}}(s,a)$ is the standard deviation of the Q-value estimate for the current state-action pair $(s,a)$, not for the next state-action pair $(s',a')$. The paper's text writes $\hat{Q}_{\mathrm{std}}(s',a')$ and $w(s,a)=\sigma(-\hat{Q}_{\mathrm{std}}(s',a')*T)+0.5$, which conflates the state-action pair used for weighting with the one used for uncertainty estimation. This is a factual error about prior work. Rewrite the sentence as: 'The weights $w(s,a)$ are computed based on a sigmoid of the negative standard deviation $\hat{Q}_{\mathrm{std}}(s,a)$ scaled by a temperature hyperparameter $T$, and then offset such that they are between 0.5 and 1: $w(s,a)=\sigma(-\hat{Q}_{\mathrm{std}}(s,a)*T)+0.5$.'

comments.title: Contradiction in description of variance sources
comments.quote: First, we use variance networks *(Kendall and Gal, 2017)*, whose loss function for regression is the negative log-likelihood instead of the L2 distance. For a given state-action pair $(s,a)$, the network learns the target’s noise, due for example to the stochasticity of the environment or the update of the policy.
comments.explanation: The text states that variance networks learn 'the target’s noise, due for example to the stochasticity of the environment or the update of the policy.' However, in Section 3.1.2 of the paper, the authors decompose the target variance into two components: aleatoric uncertainty from the environment (reward and transition noise) and epistemic uncertainty from the Q-value prediction. Variance networks trained with negative log-likelihood are designed to capture aleatoric (data-dependent) noise, not epistemic uncertainty from policy updates. The phrase 'or the update of the policy' conflates these two sources. This is internally inconsistent with the paper's own decomposition. Revise to: 'the network learns the target’s aleatoric noise, due for example to the stochasticity of the environment.'

comments.title: Sampled variance conflates epistemic and aleatoric uncertainty
comments.quote: Several networks independently train an ensemble of size $N$ that can be interpreted as a distribution over predictions. The expected behaviour is that different networks will only make similar predictions if they were sufficiently trained for a given input. The sampled variance of the networks’ outputs is thus interpreted as epistemic uncertainty.
comments.explanation: The text treats sampled variance as purely epistemic, while in standard ensemble methods (and in the paper's own later decomposition in Section 3.1.2) the variance across ensemble members captures both epistemic and aleatoric components. The paper's later framework uses a variance network to predict total target variance, not just epistemic. The quoted passage should clarify that the sampled variance is an estimate of total uncertainty, or explain how the ensemble design (e.g., bootstrapping, randomized priors) isolates epistemic uncertainty. Rewrite the quoted text as: 'The sampled variance of the networks' outputs is thus interpreted as a measure of uncertainty, which in the context of this work is used to approximate the total variance of the TD target.'

comments.title: Initial variance underestimation claim is overstated
comments.quote: When used at the very beginning of the training process, sampled ensembles present one particular challenge: as the networks are initialized, they all predict small values. The initial variance, instead of capturing the lack of knowledge, is then underestimated.
comments.explanation: The claim that 'they all predict small values' is not generally true for standard random initializations (e.g., Glorot, He). With different random seeds, ensemble outputs can vary substantially, yielding non-negligible variance. The paper does not specify the initialization scheme or provide evidence that variance is systematically underestimated. The text should acknowledge this as a potential issue rather than a guaranteed one. Rewrite as: 'When used at the very beginning of the training process, sampled ensembles may present a challenge: if the networks are initialized to produce similar small outputs, the initial variance may be low, failing to capture the lack of knowledge. Randomized Prior Functions (RPFs) address this by pairing each network with a fixed, untrained network, ensuring high variance in unexplored regions.'

comments.title: Missing justification for Bernoulli mask ensuring different training
comments.quote:
It is possible to include a random Bernoulli mask

<!-- PAGE BREAK -->

of probability $p$ to each training sample, to ensure that each network undergoes different t
comments.explanation: The text claims that a Bernoulli mask 'ensures' each network undergoes different training, but this is not guaranteed: with probability p, two networks may see the same subset of data (especially if p is close to 1 or the dataset is small). The mask only encourages diversity, it does not ensure it. The paper should clarify that the mask promotes diversity rather than guaranteeing it. Add after the quoted sentence: 'This promotes diversity in the training data seen by each network, though it does not guarantee distinct subsets.'

comments.title: Summation index should start at 1, not 0
comments.quote: $\mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{BIV}}(D,\theta)=\left(\sum_{k=0}^{K}\frac{1}{\sigma_{k}^{2}+\xi}\right)^{-1}\sum_{k=0}^{K}\frac{(f_{\theta}(x_{k})-\tilde{y}_{k})^{2}}{\sigma_{k}^{2}+\xi}$ (1)
comments.explanation: The summation index runs from $k=0$ to $K$, which would give $K+1$ terms. Standard convention for a mini-batch of size $K$ is to sum from $k=1$ to $K$. The authors likely intend $k=1$ to $K$ to match the stated mini-batch size $K$. This is a minor indexing error but could cause confusion if readers try to verify the effective batch size formula. Rewrite the sums as $\sum_{k=1}^{K}$ to be consistent with the mini-batch size $K$.

comments.title: Indexing error in BIV loss summation
comments.quote:
=\mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{BIV}}(D,\theta)+\lambda\mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{LA}}(D,\theta)$
$=\sum_{k=0}^{K}\left[\left(\sum_{j=0}^{K}\frac{1}{\gamma^{2}\sigma_{\bar{Q},j}^{2}+\xi}\right)^{-1}\frac{\left(\mu_{\bar{Q}_{\theta}}(s_{k},a_{k})-T(s_{k},a_{k})\right)^{2}}{\gamma^{2}\sigma_{\bar{Q},k}^{2}+\xi}\right.$
$\left.+\frac{\lambda}{K}\left(\frac{\left(\mu_{\bar{Q}_{\theta}}(s_{k},a_{k})-T(s_{k},a_{k})\right)^{2}}{\sigma_{\bar{Q}_{\theta}}^{2}(s_{k},a_{k})}+\ln\sigma_{\bar{Q}_{\theta}}^{2}(s_{k},a_{k})\right)\right]$ (10)
comments.explanation: The outer sum runs from $k=0$ to $K$, which yields $K+1$ terms, but the minibatch is stated to have size $K$. This is a genuine off-by-one indexing error. The intended sum is over $K$ elements, so the outer sum should run from $k=1$ to $K$ or $k=0$ to $K-1$. Rewrite the outer sum as $\sum_{k=1}^{K}$ or $\sum_{k=0}^{K-1}$ to match the minibatch size $K$.

comments.title: Ambiguous seed count description
comments.quote: Unless specified otherwise, each result is the average of runs over 25 seeds: 5 for the environment $\times$ 5 for network initialization.
comments.explanation: The description of the 25 seeds is ambiguous. The phrase '5 for the environment × 5 for network initialization' suggests a crossed design, but it is unclear what '5 for the environment' means — environments are not typically seeded separately from network initialization. More likely, the authors mean 5 random seeds for the environment (controlling the random number generator for the environment dynamics) and 5 random seeds for the network initialization, giving 5×5 = 25 total runs. However, this interpretation is not standard; usually one seed controls both. The authors should clarify whether they are using a full factorial design (5 environment seeds × 5 initialization seeds) or simply 25 independent runs with different random seeds. If it is a factorial design, they should explain why this is necessary and how the results are aggregated (e.g., averaging over both factors). Rewrite as: 'Each result is the average over 25 independent runs, using 5 different random seeds for the environment and 5 different random seeds for network initialization in a full factorial design.'

comments.title: Claim about low epistemic uncertainty in Cartpole-Noise is unsupported
comments.quote: In Cartpole-Noise, var-ensembles carry most of the improvement, maybe because the low amount of states leads to low epistemic uncertainty.
comments.explanation: The authors speculate that var-ensembles dominate the improvement in Cartpole-Noise because 'the low amount of states leads to low epistemic uncertainty.' However, Cartpole-Noise is a continuous state environment (position, velocity, angle, angular velocity) — it does not have a 'low amount of states' in the discrete sense. The authors may be referring to the fact that the environment has a small state space relative to Atari, but this is not quantified. Moreover, the relationship between state-space size and epistemic uncertainty is not straightforward: epistemic uncertainty also depends on the coverage of the replay buffer and the function approximation capacity. The paper provides no evidence (e.g., variance estimates over training, coverage metrics) to support this claim. Remove or rephrase the speculation, or add empirical evidence such as a plot of the average variance estimates over training for each environment.

comments.title: Overclaim on BIV performance in supervised learning
comments.quote: We then use Batch Inverse-Variance (BIV) *(Mai et al., 2021)*, which has been shown to significantly improve the performance of supervised learning with neural networks in the case of heteroscedastic regression.
comments.explanation: The claim that BIV 'has been shown to significantly improve the performance of supervised learning with neural networks in the case of heteroscedastic regression' is an overstatement. The cited paper (Mai et al., 2021) is a workshop paper (ICML Workshop on Uncertainty & Robustness in Deep Learning) that proposes BIV and shows improvements on a limited set of synthetic and UCI regression benchmarks. It does not establish BIV as a broadly validated method for heteroscedastic regression with neural networks. The paper should qualify this claim to reflect the preliminary nature of the evidence, e.g., 'has been shown to improve performance on several heteroscedastic regression benchmarks.'

comments.title: Unsubstantiated claim about model-based RL
comments.quote: Model-based, active or continuous learning could also benefit from the underlying ideas presented in this paper.
comments.explanation: The paper speculates that model-based, active, or continuous learning could benefit from the ideas, but provides no evidence or reasoning to support this. The method relies on variance estimation from ensembles and inverse-variance weighting, which may not transfer straightforwardly to model-based settings where the uncertainty structure is different. This claim is unsupported and should be removed or tempered with a caveat, e.g., 'We speculate that the underlying ideas may also be applicable to model-based or active learning settings, though this remains to be verified.'

coarse__glm-4.7-flash.label: Coarse (glm-4.7-flash)
coarse__glm-4.7-flash.overall_feedback:
The paper proposes Inverse Variance Reinforcement Learning (IV-RL), a framework that combines Batch Inverse-Variance (BIV) weighting with variance ensembles to mitigate the impact of noisy value supervision in model-free DRL. The authors demonstrate significant improvements in sample efficiency for both discrete (DQN) and continuous (SAC) control tasks.

The paper presents a technically sound approach that extends heteroscedastic regression techniques to the RL domain. The theoretical decomposition of target variance into predictive and policy/environmental components is a clear contribution. However, the empirical evaluation, while extensive, lacks a critical analysis of the computational overhead and a direct comparison with state-of-the-art uncertainty-aware methods on a broader set of benchmarks. The paper's framing as a general framework is slightly undermined by the heavy reliance on specific ensemble architectures.

Overview issues:

- Computational Overhead of Ensembles: The paper introduces a framework that relies heavily on ensembles (variance ensembles) and variance networks, which significantly increases the computational cost compared to standard single-network baselines. While the authors mention this in Appendix D, the main text does not adequately discuss the trade-off between sample efficiency gains and increased computational expense. A top-tier venue would expect a quantitative analysis of the wall-clock time or FLOPs required to train IV-RL versus the baselines, especially since sample efficiency is often traded for computational efficiency in real-world applications. The current lack of a clear cost-benefit analysis weakens the practical relevance of the contribution.

- Lack of Comparison with State-of-the-Art Uncertainty Methods: The paper compares IV-RL against BootstrapDQN, EnsembleSAC, and SUNRISE. However, SUNRISE is a relatively early method from 2021, and there are more recent state-of-the-art approaches in uncertainty-aware RL, such as Bayesian Policy Optimization (BPO) or recent advancements in ensemble-based exploration (e.g., using randomized priors more extensively). The paper does not explicitly state why these more recent methods were not included in the comparison. Without benchmarking against the current SOTA in uncertainty-aware RL, it is difficult to assess whether the improvements are marginal or substantial relative to the most advanced techniques available today.

- Theoretical Justification for Combining BIV and LA: The paper argues that the combination of BIV (equation 10) and Loss Attenuation (LA, equation 3) is beneficial because they address different sources of variance: BIV targets the variance of the target $T(s,a)$ estimated by the var-ensemble, while LA targets the variance of the critic's prediction $ ar{Q}(s,a)$. However, the theoretical justification for why these two losses can be simply added with a scalar hyperparameter $	ext{	extbackslash lambda}$ is not rigorously established. The interaction between the two weighting schemes could potentially interfere with each other (e.g., one scheme might assign low weight to a sample, making the other scheme's gradient noisy). A more formal analysis or a clearer explanation of why the combination is stable is needed to support the claim that they are 'complementary'.

- Scope Limitations in Exploration Scenarios: The paper shows that IV-RL does not improve sample efficiency in the MountainCar environment (Section 4.1), stating that there is 'very little additional information in every other state for IV-DQN to improve over BootstrapDQN.' This observation highlights a potential limitation: IV-RL is designed to handle noisy supervision, but it may not be effective in scenarios where the primary challenge is pure exploration (i.e., finding a state with a non-zero reward). The paper should discuss this limitation more explicitly. Furthermore, the authors rely on BootstrapDQN for exploration in discrete tasks (Section 2.3), which itself uses ensembles. It is unclear how IV-RL's weighting interacts with the exploration bonus derived from the same ensembles. A clearer separation of concerns between the exploration strategy and the value estimation weighting would strengthen the methodology.

- Missing concrete parametric example: The paper derives a complex loss function and variance decomposition but provides no fully-specified parametric model to illustrate it. Readers need a concrete setting—such as a simple linear environment or a specific stochastic dynamics model—to verify the theoretical claims about the decomposition of variance into predictive and environment-induced components. Without this, the mathematical framework remains abstract and difficult to connect to actual behavior.

- Missing simulation of variance calibration: The paper relies on the claim that variance networks and ensembles are well-calibrated, yet it does not present a calibration study. A standard requirement for uncertainty methods is to demonstrate that the estimated variance correlates with actual prediction error. A simulation showing the relationship between predicted variance and actual loss across different training stages would validate the theoretical assumptions and strengthen the methodology.

- Missing implementation details for hyperparameters: The paper mentions tuning hyperparameters like $	extbackslash xi$ and $	extbackslash lambda$ but provides no guidance on their selection or sensitivity. For a method intended for practical application, a discussion of how to set these values in new environments or how to transfer them across tasks is necessary. Without this, the algorithm remains a theoretical proposal rather than a practical tool.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Add a quantitative analysis of the computational overhead (wall-clock time or FLOPs) of training IV-RL compared to the baselines, and discuss the trade-off between sample efficiency and computational cost.

- Include comparisons with more recent state-of-the-art uncertainty-aware RL methods (e.g., BPO) to better contextualize the improvements.

- Provide a more rigorous theoretical justification or analysis of the stability and interaction between the BIV and LA loss components in the combined objective function.

- Explicitly discuss the limitations of the method in pure exploration scenarios (like MountainCar) and clarify the interaction between IV-RL's weighting and the ensemble-based exploration bonuses used in the baselines.

comments.title: Misleading standard error reporting
comments.quote: Table 2: Performance at 100K and 200K time steps (100 and 200 episodes) for several robotics environments in OpenAI Gym. The results show the mean and standard error over 25 runs.
comments.explanation: Table 2 reports standard errors, but the values shown (e.g., $ ackslash pm 187$) appear to be standard deviations. For 25 runs, the standard error should be the standard deviation divided by 5. Please verify and correct the table to ensure the reported values match the caption.

comments.title: Missing repository version information
comments.quote: To allow reproducibility of our results, the code, as well as the hyperparameters used to produce each result presented in this paper, are available at https://github.com/montrealrobotics/iv_rl.
comments.explanation: The reproducibility statement links to code, but does not specify which version corresponds to the results. To ensure reproducibility, the authors should tag the repository or provide a specific commit hash.

comments.title: Ambiguous environment specifications
comments.quote: The environments and implementation we used (OpenAI Gym, BSuite, MBBL) are all publicly accessible, although a Mujoco license is needed to run some of them.
comments.explanation: The reproducibility statement lists libraries (OpenAI Gym, BSuite, MBBL) but omits the specific environment names or IDs used. This ambiguity prevents readers from verifying the exact experimental setup.

comments.title: Ambiguous explanation for Cartpole-Noise
comments.quote: In Cartpole-Noise, var-ensembles carry most of the improvement, maybe because the low amount of states leads to low epistemic uncertainty.
comments.explanation: The explanation for why var-ensembles help in Cartpole-Noise is unclear. The phrase 'low amount of states' is ambiguous; the authors should clarify whether they mean a small state space or a small dataset size.

comments.title: Vague definition of epistemic uncertainty
comments.quote: Epistemic uncertainty is instead related to the trained model: it depends on the information available in the training data, the model’s capacity to retain it, and the learning algorithm *(Hüllermeier and Waegeman, 2021)*.
comments.explanation: The definition of epistemic uncertainty in section 2.2.1 conflates model uncertainty with the lack of knowledge. A clearer definition would emphasize that epistemic uncertainty reflects the model's ignorance about the true function, which diminishes as more data is acquired.

comments.title: Missing computational resource acknowledgement
comments.quote: authors wish to thank the DEEL project CRDPJ 537462-18 funded by the National Science and Engineering Research Council of Canada (NSERC) and the
comments.explanation: Given the significant computational cost of the ensemble-based methods, the acknowledgements section should explicitly acknowledge the computational resources required to train these models.
